coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation of thermal runaway behaviour and inhibition strategies in large-capacity lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries for electric vehicles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Cheng
Wang, Ruijie
coredata.dc:description: Large-scale lithium-ion batteries play an important role in the electric vehicle market and the thermal runaway (TR) behaviour of batteries is one of the significant threats to the passengers. In this study, we conducted a series of thermal abuse tests concerning single battery and battery box to investigate the TR behaviour of a large-capacity (310 Ah) lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) battery and the TR inhibition effects of different extinguishing agents. The study shows that before the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film, temperature consistency within the battery were maintained and it was advisable to use temperature detectors. With the destruction of the internal structure of the battery and the uneven temperature distribution at the subsequent battery reaction stage, a large amount of combustible gas was released when the safety valve was opened. The combustible gas fire detector is used to improve alarm reliability and provided a stable early warning response time. Based on this TR behaviour, a LiFePO4 battery box with a standard size of 1060 × 660 × 250 mm was constructed. The TR inhibition effect of two different extinguishing agents (heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone) were investigated using a metal tube spraying method with a spraying dose of 1.8 kg (spraying rate of 0.06 kg/s) and a spraying pressure of 2.5 MPa. The results showed that using either of these, satisfy the requirements for TR inhibition in large-capacity LiFePO4 batteries and the flames could be completely extinguished without any reignition, and the chain reaction of the surrounding batteries was effectively prevented. Moreover, heptafluoropropane was proven to have a shorter fire-extinguishing time, whereas perfluorohexanone had the advantage of inhibiting temperature rise. This study provides valuable experimental results for early warning and inhibition of TR in large-capacity LiFePO4 batteries for electric vehicles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
Thermal runaway
Heptafluoropropane
Perfluorohexanone
Inhibition effect

Experimental result and analysis of the effect of the heptafluoropropane fire-extinguishing agent

Comparison of the inhibition effects of heptafluoropropane and perfluorochione fire-extinguishing agents

Schematic diagram of the single battery experimental platform.

Extinguishing-agent suppression experiment of power battery box thermal runaway behaviour. (a) Before thermal runaway. (b)When thermal runaway occurs. (c) Open flame ignition. (d) After fire extinguishing and reignition.
Temperature change curves of the LIB TR test after spraying the heptafluoropropane fire-extinguishing agent.

row.entry: Extinguishing time after spraying the suppression medium
Cooling rate at the initial stage of fire extinguishing
The maximum value of temperature after spraying the suppression medium

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation of thermal runaway behaviour and inhibition strategies in large-capacity lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries for electric vehicles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Large-scale lithium-ion batteries play an important role in the electric vehicle market and the thermal runaway (TR) behaviour of batteries is one of the significant threats to the passengers. In this study, we conducted a series of thermal abuse tests concerning single battery and battery box to investigate the TR behaviour of a large-capacity (310 Ah) lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) battery and the TR inhibition effects of different extinguishing agents. The study shows that before the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film, temperature consistency within the battery were maintained and it was advisable to use temperature detectors. With the destruction of the internal structure of the battery and the uneven temperature distribution at the subsequent battery reaction stage, a large amount of combustible gas was released when the safety valve was opened. The combustible gas fire detector is used to improve alarm reliability and provided a stable early warning response time. Based on this TR behaviour, a LiFePO4 battery box with a standard size of 1060 × 660 × 250 mm was constructed. The TR inhibition effect of two different extinguishing agents (heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone) were investigated using a metal tube spraying method with a spraying dose of 1.8 kg (spraying rate of 0.06 kg/s) and a spraying pressure of 2.5 MPa. The results showed that using either of these, satisfy the requirements for TR inhibition in large-capacity LiFePO4 batteries and the flames could be completely extinguished without any reignition, and the chain reaction of the surrounding batteries was effectively prevented. Moreover, heptafluoropropane was proven to have a shorter fire-extinguishing time, whereas perfluorohexanone had the advantage of inhibiting temperature rise. This study provides valuable experimental results for early warning and inhibition of TR in large-capacity LiFePO4 batteries for electric vehicles.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely used in the electric vehicle market owing to their high energy density, long lifespan, and low self-discharge rate [1–3]. However, an increasing number of LIB combustion and explosion cases have been reported because of the instability of battery materials at high temperatures and under abuse conditions, such as thermal [4,5], electrical, and mechanical abuse [6–8]. An overcharge test of LIBs by Wang et al. [9] showed that an increase in the Ni content could lead to worse thermal stability, and thus, a higher risk of thermal runaway (TR) of the battery. Liu et al. [10] reported that when the surface temperature of a lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) battery exceeds 150 ℃, there is a high risk of TR along with the release of a large amount of combustible gas. The gas burns when exposed to an open flame, leading to a more severe TR of the battery at high ambient temperatures [11]. However, current research mainly focuses on small capacity batteries. The utilization of large capacity LiFePO4 is emerging as a significant trend due to the continuously growing demand for higher power density. Additionally, the specific scenarios of extinguishing agents, particular in electrical vehicles, have not been thoroughly examined. Therefore, studying the TR behaviour of large capacity LIBs used for electrical vehicles is of great practical significance.

Studies on TR inhibition in LIBs have attracted significant attention [12–14]. Li et al. [15] attempted to extinguish the open flames caused by the TR of LIBs using ABC dry powder, carbon dioxide, foam, and other fire-extinguishing agents. Their results showed that although the abovementioned fire-extinguishing agents could inhibit fire, reignition still occurred. Zhang et al. [16] compared the inhibitory effects of ultrafine water mist and dry ultrafine powder on LIB fires. The results showed that the ultrafine water mist had a better extinguishing effect and stronger fire inhibition ability and could extinguish the battery fire in a short time and quickly lower the battery surface temperature. By conducting fire-extinguishing experiments on 38 Ah LIBs with a variety of fire-extinguishing agents, Liu et al. [17] proved that water, perfluorohexanone and heptafluoropropane were more stable than carbon dioxide fire-extinguishing agents. Zhang et al. [18] reported that a combination of various fire-extinguishing agents was more effective than a single fire-extinguishing agent.

Most importantly, existing studies on the TR behaviour of LIBs have mainly focused on comparing the battery fire mechanism and the universal fire-extinguishing performance of different fire-extinguishing agents [19,20]. Nevertheless, due to their variety and scenarios, no consensus on the selection of extinguishing agents has been reached [21]. The environmental effects of the battery materials and possible reaction products should also be considered [22]. It is widely believed that within the vehicular filed, several factors should be considered including the ease of usage for fire extinguishers and the potential risk of damage to surrounding devices after their deployment. Perfluorohexanone and heptafluoropropane are the two primary fire distinguishing agents considered for fire suppression in LiFePO4 batteries, particularly in the context of electric vehicles. The study analyses the changes in battery temperature and voltage during the TR process, the fire detector and fire-extinguishing agent spraying times were evaluated. A simulation experimental platform for standard electrical vehicle LiFePO4 power battery box with a ratio of 1:1 was constructed to study the influence of two gas fire-extinguishing agents, perfluorohexanone and heptafluoropropane, on the temperature field of the battery box. These results can serve as a scientific reference for the early warning and inhibition of TR in large-capacity LiFePO4 batteries.

Mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuses are the three most common external causes of battery TR. Many accident investigation results have shown that the proportion of accidents caused by mechanical abuse is relatively small and can be improved by human intervention. Battery heat includes the heat provided by the external heat source
Q
in
and the self-generating heat
Q
cell
, and the relationship between these can be expressed as [23]
(1)
Q
in
= ω Δ t#
where ω is the heating power of the electrical heating plate, which is 800 W, and
Δ t
is the time variation.
Q
cell
is composed of the reaction heat
Q
r
, Joule heat
Q
j
, polarization heat
Q
p
, and side reaction heat
Q
side
. This can be formulated as follows:
(2)
Q
cell
=
Q
r
+
Q
j
+
Q
p
+
Q
side
#

When TR occurs in LIBs,
Q
cell
is dominated by the side reaction heat,
Q
side
, which can be classified into four parts [12]:
(3)
Q
cell
≈
Q
side
=
Q
SEI
+
Q
an
+
Q
ca
+
Q
ele
#

The decomposition heat of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film Q SEI [24] is written as
(4)
Q
SEI
=
H
SEI
m
SEI
R
SEI
#

Heat released from the reaction between the anode and electrolyte
Q
an
is written as
(5)
Q
an
=
H
an
m
an
R
an
#

Heat released from the reaction between the cathode and electrolyte
Q
ca
is written as
(6)
Q
ca
=
H
ca
m
ca
R
ca
#

The decomposition heat of the electrolyte
Q
ele
is written as
(7)
Q
ele
=
H
ele
m
ele
R
ele
#

In Eqs. (4)–(8),
H
SEI
,
H
an
,
H
ca
, and
H
ele
are the enthalpies of chemical reaction formation,
m
SEI
,
m
an
,
m
ca
, and
m
ele
are the masses of each material, and
R
SEI
,
R
an
,
R
ca
, and
R
ele
are the decomposition reaction rates, respectively.

At the same time, the battery will continuously dissipate heat
Q
diss
outwards:
(8)
Q
diss
=
Q
cond
+
Q
rad
+
Q
conv
+
Q
gas
#
where
Q
cond
,
Q
rad
, and
Q
conv
represent the heat dissipations of heat conduction, radiation, and convection, respectively, and
Q
gas
is the heat loss caused by the battery exhaust gas.

According to Eq. (4) and the related literature [25],
Q
an
is related to the Li+ concentration intercalated in the anode, which means
Q
an
is directly proportional to the state of charge (SOC) of the battery. When the SOC of an LIB reaches 100 %, TR becomes more severe and dangerous. Therefore, it is imperative to study battery TR behaviour with an SOC of 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Platform construction and experiment design of single battery experiment
ce:section.ce:para: A large-capacity single LiFePO4 battery of 310 Ah with a size of 174 × 54 × 207 mm and a nominal voltage of 3.2 V was investigated in this study. Fig. 1 shows the device designed to investigate the temperature and voltage variation characteristics during the TR of the battery. Two hard splints were used to fix the LiFePO4 battery, with an 800 W electric heating plate placed on one side. To reduce the influence of heat transfer among the heating plate, battery, and metal fixture, a heat-insulation panel was used to separate the heating plate and battery from the metal fixture. To ensure more accurate temperature collection, DAM-TC16-N thermocouples T1–T3 were set at the cathode and anode tabs of the upper surface of the battery and opposite the centre of the electric heating plate. As shown in Fig. 2, the experimental platform was equipped with an explosion-proof box, battery charging discharging cabinet, cloud thermal image Foric 222s-1 infrared imager, and GC-P100AC high-speed camera to monitor the temperature and voltage changes of the LiFePO4 battery. All experiments were conducted in the explosion-proof box, where various characteristic gas sensors were constructed to measure the gas production during the battery TR process in real time.

Based on the mechanism of single battery TR behaviour and experimental platform, the experimental simulations were performed as follows. First, the battery was charged to a 100 % SOC by a constant-current charging mode. Subsequently, it was set to rest for 1 h and then charged under 1 C constant current of 310 A for 12 min, according to the corresponding standard. However, the charging procedure was closed using protective devices at 11 min 13 s. Overcharging induces electrical abuse which may cause more severe TR. The battery was then heated using an electric heating plate with a power of 800 W to simulate thermal abuse. According to Zhu [25], when TR was induced at different heating positions, the time for TR caused by heating at the side position was shorter than that at the bottom position. Thus, heating was performed from the side position in this study using the electric heating plate. The electric heating device was turned off when the T3 temperature point reached 300 ℃ [26].

As shown in Fig. 3, the experimental platform contained an LIB box and a fire-extinguishing device, and the latter was connected to the power battery box through a metal tube which is the agent channel. A data acquisition communication line was connected between the fire-extinguishing device and power battery box. The data acquired in the battery box were transmitted to the main controller in the fire-extinguishing device.

To simulate the standard battery box, which is commonly used in electric vehicle applications, a test battery box that coincided with the typical specifications and dimensions in GB/T34013–2017 was designed with dimensions of 1060 × 660 × 250 mm. The top cover of battery box was loaded with an explosion-proof glass window for observation. As shown in Fig. 4, there were 45 batteries, including 36 model batteries and 9 LiFePO4 square aluminium shell batteries of 310 Ah. The battery size was 174 × 54 × 207 mm, and the safe blast pressure was 0.8 MPa. To prolong the spraying time of the fire-extinguishing agent and uniformly deliver the working medium to different parts of the battery box, a rubber tube with a length of 760 mm and an inner diameter of Φ8 mm containing openings on both sides (Φ2.0 mm × 10) was used to spray the fire-extinguishing agent. In the test, the 310Ah battery in the middle of the right side is used to conduct TR simulation. An electric heating device of 800 W was installed in the middle of the right side of the TR battery, which could be controlled externally. To better detect the temperature, 8 K-type thermocouple temperature sensors (T4–T11) were arranged on middle of the side of the battery and two same sensors (T12, T13) were arranged at the top of the two batteries which were closely installed. In addition, 10 sets of electric tungsten wire ignition devices were arranged in the middle of the battery box 15 mm above the batteries, which could also be manually and externally manipulated. Simultaneously, the platform employed infrared imagers and high-speed cameras to monitor and record the entire process.

The fire-extinguishing device mainly included fire suppression, fire detection and alarm, and logic control units. The fire suppression unit consisted of a shell, fire extinguisher storage tank, pneumatic pressure switch, container valve, total electric explosion valve, sub-electric explosion valve, and distributor, whose internal structure is shown in Fig. 5. The fire detection and alarm unit were composed of a cable-type fusible temperature sensor, combustible gas concentration sensor, and dual alarm switch for temperature and light control. Meanwhile, the fire-extinguishing device offered self-detection functions such as the storage pressure alarm of the fire extinguisher storage tank, battery feed detection and alarm, and communication failure alarm through the logic control unit, which automatically identified and extinguished the fire.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental result and analysis of single LiFePO4 battery
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in temperature points T1–T3 (Fig. 1) and the voltage of the battery over time were recorded, as shown in Fig. 6. The battery TR process could be divided into five stages:

Stage I: Resting period. The total rest period lasted for 7200 s, and the last 60 s were used for illustration. After charging to a 100 % SOC, the battery remained in a resting state. At this stage, the shape, internal electrical parameters, and temperature of the battery exhibited no obvious changes. The voltage was kept around 3.35 V, and the temperature at the three measuring points were essentially the same (at room temperature of 25±2 ℃). The battery was in a safe period, and all the battery components remained stable.

Stage Ⅱ: SEI film decomposition (at approximately 60–700 s). The voltage increased steadily to approximately 6.00 V owing to the constant-current charging, whereas the surface temperature of the battery slowly increased to 80 ℃. The temperature at the three measuring points were essentially the same. At approximately 700 s, the SEI film in the battery began to decompose. From Eq. (4), it can be inferred that the heat Q SEI released by SEI decomposition was not too high because the SEI film was thin, light, and had low chemical activity. However, in this situation, the anode was no longer protected by the SEI film. This reaction is of great significance for the TR of LIBs.

Stage Ⅲ: Anode reaction with the electrolyte (at approximately 700–950 s). The intercalated Li in the anode reacted violently with the electrolyte and released heat. Consequently, the T3 rate of increase suddenly increased and exceeded those of T1 and T2 in approximately 710 s. The battery temperature increased rapidly from 84.07 to 94.68 ℃ in 10 s with a reaction rate of approximately 1 ℃/s. In the subsequent 20 s, the battery temperature reached 100.40 ℃. T1 and T2 also increased slowly, but they clearly differed from that of T3. Moreover, the uneven deposition of Li led to the formation of Li dendrites.

As the internal temperature of the battery increased, the resistance of the positive temperature coefficient (PTC), which is a thermistor with a positive temperature coefficient, increased, leading to an open circuit in the battery. The highest measured voltage (16.29 V) was the instantaneous voltage of the charging devices. The charging procedure was terminated when the voltage exceeded the threshold protection voltage of the charging devices (15 V), then the PTC resumed, and the voltage decreased to 3.42 V. However, because of overcharging, the battery diaphragm started to shrink, and a partial short-cut occurred, which resulted in a voltage drop to 0 at 950 s.

Stage Ⅳ: Cathode reaction and electrolyte (at approximately 950–1200 s). At 950 s, the battery safety valve was opened, and a large amount of gas and smoke was ejected, with the battery voltage decaying to 0 V. From Eq. (9), it can be inferred that owing to the heat loss caused by gas ejection from the battery, T3 slowly increased to 140 ℃. However, the violent reaction between electrodes and electrolyte led to accelerated rates of increase for T1 and T2 to exceed T3 to approximately 250 ℃ at a high temperature increase rate of 0.59 ℃/s. At this stage, the oxygen produced by the reaction between the cathode and electrolyte continued to react with the solvent, which also released heat. The Li dendrites at the anode continued to grow until the separator was pierced. This caused a severe short-circuit in the battery and destroyed its internal structure.

Stage Ⅴ: TR period (after 1200 s). At this stage, the electrolyte became unstable. A lot of heat was released due to the reaction at 1200 s. T3 increased rapidly and exceeded T1 and T2, reaching the highest temperature of 390.99 ℃ at 1510 s with a temperature increase rate of 0.84 ℃/s. According to Eq. (8), owing to the highly active chemical properties of the electrolyte,
Q
ele
produced by electrolyte decomposition was large at this stage. This resulted in severe battery TR. The temperatures of T1 and T2 were 286.37 ℃ and 291.71 ℃, respectively, indicating that the battery was in an unsafe state.

According to the above analysis, before the decomposition of the SEI film, the LiFePO4 battery components were maintained in a stable condition with a high consistency of temperatures in each part. In addition, the battery exhibited relatively good performance. When the battery temperature reached approximately 80 ℃, a direct contact between the anode and electrolyte was created by the decomposition of the SEI film, which led to a series of severe side reactions and thus the release of considerable heat. The temperature consistency in each part of the battery was satisfactory during this stage. When the surface temperature of the battery increased to 80 ℃, the thermal detector could be used for early warning and corresponding actions could be taken to ensure the battery safety. With an increase in the temperature and destruction of the components in the battery, the temperature distribution inside the battery was extremely uneven. At this point, the use of temperature sensors to detect fires can lead to excessive errors and misjudgements. Mathieu et al. [26] reported that the final gas mixture during the LIB TR process was composed of C3H8, C2H6, C2H4, CH4, H2, CO, and CO2. In our study, the gas escaping from the LiFePO4 battery was monitored in real time, and a variety of flammable and explosive gases such as CO, H2, CH4, C2H2, and C2H4 were produced during the TR process. The gas can cause a significant danger of combustion or even explosion when exposed to an open flame. It is advisable to use a gas fire detector to provide a fire alarm when a large amount of combustible gas and smoke is ejected with the safety valve open. Koch et al. [27] also believed that the characteristic gas sensor had the fastest and most efficient response and high signal recognition, making it suitable for early warning of TR in LIBs. They also observed that the coupling of multiple gas sensors performed the best. Owing to the heat loss caused by the large amount of gas ejected from the battery at this stage, the battery temperature did not increase quickly at the beginning. There was a relatively stable time interval between the opening of the battery safety valve and complete TR of the battery. By monitoring the opening behaviour of the battery safety valve, a stable early warning response time can be reserved before complete TR of the battery, providing time for the safe evacuation of people and implementation of fire control measures [28,29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental plan and observation of influence of the temperature field on the power LiFePO4 battery box
ce:section.ce:para: The battery was first heated until the battery safety valve opened, and a large amount of gas and smoke was ejected, after which the electric tungsten wire was connected to artificially induce an open flame. Heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agents with doses of 1.8 kg (spray rate of 0.06 kg/s) were used to suppress the TR of the LIBs [30] using the hose-spraying method. Observations from the beginning are shown in Fig. 7. Even after the fire was extinguished, the battery remains susceptible to the potential occurrence of TR chain reaction. Additionally, the combustible gas may also lead to possible reignition. The electric tungsten wire ignition device was switched on every 3 min over 30 min to detect whether reignition occurred in the battery box (3 s at a time). During the experiment, the temperatures of the battery box and batteries were monitored using video recordings and temperature sensors.

From the heat flow formula [14], it is known that：
(9)
∅ = − λ A
Δ T
δ
#
where λ is the heat conductivity coefficient of the battery, A is the contact area between the batteries, δ is the thickness of the battery shell, and
Δ T
is the temperature difference between the batteries. This indicates that the direction of heat transfer opposites to that of the temperature gradient.

As shown in Fig. 5, the contact area adjacent to the left side of the heated battery was 174 × 207 mm2, and the contact areas adjacent to the upper and lower sides were 207 × 54 mm2. It was more representative to study the battery on the left side to indicate the influence of the TR battery on surrounding batteries, which had the largest contact area with the heated battery while maintaining the same λ and δ . In this case, the influence of
Δ T
(T12–T13) on the heat transfer of the heated battery was explored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental result and analysis of the temperature field on the power LiFePO4 battery box concerning two extinguishing agents
Experimental result and analysis of the effect of the heptafluoropropane fire-extinguishing agent
ce:section.ce:para: The change in the temperature field of the battery box after spraying with the heptafluoropropane fire-extinguishing agent is shown in Fig. 8.

As is shown in Fig. 8, when the temperature of the heating surface (T4) reached 288.6 ℃ and the temperature opposite to the heating surface (T6) reached 76.6 ℃, TR occurred with the safety valve of the LIB opened, and a large amount of gas and smoke was simultaneously ejected. The heating device was turned off immediately and an open flame was artificially induced. If the fire continued to burn for 3 min, the fire-extinguishing device activated manually. The dose of the heptafluoropropane fire-extinguishing agent was 1.8 kg. At this time, because the heating device was turned off and a large amount of gas was ejected (which decreased the heat
Q
gas
), the temperature of T4 dropped to 229.4 ℃. However, the temperature of T6 continued increasing to 83.7 ℃, indicating the persistent occurrence of TR. The fire was extinguished after 30 s, accompanied by a linear downward trend in the temperature. T4 decreased for 1080 s to 150 ℃ with a cooling rate of 0.07 ℃/s. However, after the fire was extinguished, the battery box did not reignite 10 times during the electric tungsten wire ignition processes. Li [31] studied TR in the battery box and the use of a metal tube with fine holes to spray heptafluoropropane evenly at an appropriate dosage and pressure. The results proved that heptafluoropropane could effectively control TR and prevent reignition.

Based on the results and existing research, the extinguishing mechanisms of heptafluoropropane can be classified based on two aspects. The first is the physical aspect: heptafluoropropane has a low boiling point, absorbs heat, and forms an inert gas layer. The second is the chemical aspect: the fraction of heptafluoropropane is large, and some of the bonds in the molecule break and absorb heat during fire extinguishing, which inhibits combustion active groups, such as free radicals, and reduces the temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental result and analysis of the effect of the perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agent
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in the temperature field of the battery box after the perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agent was sprayed are shown in Fig. 9.

As is shown in Fig. 9, when the safety valve was opened, the heating surface temperature (T4) of the LIB was 260.4 ℃, whereas the temperature opposite to the heating surface side (T6) was 63.5 ℃. Thereafter, the heating device was turned off immediately and an open flame was artificially induced. Meanwhile, T4 decreased slightly to 234.5 ℃, whereas T6 continued to increase to 71.4 ℃, indicating a sustainable TR process. A perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agent (1.8 kg) was immediately sprayed to suppress the TR of the battery. The fire was extinguished after 40 s, and T4 exhibited a linear downward trend. The time for T4 to descend to 150 ℃ was 510 s, with a cooling rate of 0.17 ℃/s. The battery box did not reignite 10 times during the electric tungsten wire ignition processes after the fire was extinguished.

In addition to the extinguishing mechanism of heptafluoropropane, perfluorohexanone can react with oxygen to form incombustible fluoride and water vapour, which can significantly weaken fire source sustainability. Additionally, the heat absorption performance of the higher-heat stabiliser allows it to absorb more heat in a relatively short period of time, helping to extinguish the flame and burning area quickly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the inhibition effects of heptafluoropropane and perfluorochione fire-extinguishing agents
ce:section.ce:para: For the standard battery box used in this experiment, the spraying method used a spraying dose of 1.8 kg (spraying rate of 0.06 kg/s) and spraying pressure of 2.5 MP on both sides of the metal pipe. Heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agents inhibited the TR of LIBs. The experimental results are listed in Table 1.

The above results indicate that for the 310 Ah LiFePO4 battery used in this experiment, the heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agents had noticeable inhibitory effects on TR, which extinguished open flames and inhibited reignition. Thus, for the TR of large-capacity LiFePO4 batteries, heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agents could be considered for suppression. They showed significant differences in terms of temperature and time changes after spraying with the suppression media. Although the fire-extinguishing mechanism was a synergistic effect of chemical inhibition and physical cooling, the specific working mechanisms were different.

Owing to the physical state of heptafluoropropane (gaseous) at normal temperature and pressure, it vaporised rapidly after being ejected from the pressure tank and diffused evenly and quickly in the battery box. Consequently, there was a rapid decrease in the temperature at the upper surface of the TR battery. Simultaneously, heptafluoropropane extinguished the open flame within 30 s, which was shorter than the time for perfluorohexanone. Hence, perfluorohexanone was liquid at room temperature and had a boiling point of 49 ℃. The inhibitory medium existed in a liquid state during the initial stage of spraying through the conveying pipeline, and the initial diffusion rate was not as fast as that of heptafluoropropane. Although there was an initial decrease and subsequent increase in the upper surface, this behaviour was not as obvious as that of heptafluoropropane. Moreover, the time required for the flame to be extinguished by perfluorohexanone was 40 s, which was longer than by heptafluoropropane. Owing to the endothermic vaporisation of perfluorohexanone, it had a better effect on continuous cooling and temperature rise inhibition. The cooling rate of perfluorohexanone was significantly higher than that of heptafluoropropane, and the maximum temperature of the upper surface after fire extinguishing was significantly lower than that of heptafluoropropane. Ping et al. [32] compared the performance of heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone, proving that perfluorohexanone caused no environmental damage and had a high safety margin and good fire-extinguishing effect but released more harmful gas than heptafluoropropane above 500 ℃. As a clean-gas fire-extinguishing agent, heptafluoropropane has low toxicity and high insulation; however, it also has a greenhouse effect. The results clearly showed the fire-extinguishing efficacy of perfluorohexanone and heptafluoropropane, particularly under standard battery box conditions, which have received limited attention in current research. These findings provide more scientific and comprehensive evidence for corresponding designers.

In this study, the TR behaviour of a 310 Ah LiFePO4 single battery was investigated. Consequently, a power battery simulation test box was constructed, and relevant fire-extinguishing experiments were conducted using heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone as fire-extinguishing agents.
(1)
Before the decomposition of the SEI film on the anode of the LiFePO4 battery, the temperature fire detector should be used due to the uniform internal temperature. The temperature of 80 °C is set as the critical temperature of the decomposition of the SEI film. After the decomposition of the SEI film, deploying only the temperature are highly likely to cause misjudgement owing to the rapidly changing temperature. When the battery safety valve open, large amounts of combustible gas and smoke were emitted. A combustible gas fire detector can be used to monitor the opening behaviour of the battery safety valve and reserve a stable early warning response time before the completion of the TR process.
(2)
For the standard battery box commonly used in electrical vehicles, heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone fire-extinguishing agents were studied. The results revealed that both heptafluoropropane and perfluorohexanone can effectively extinguish fire without reignition and prevent chain reactions in the surrounding batteries. By comparing the experimental results and extinguishing mechanisms of both agents, the cooling rate of perfluorohexanone (0.17 ℃/s) is significantly higher than that of heptafluoropropane (0.07 ℃/s). Meanwhile, the extinguishing time of heptafluoropropane was found to be 10 s shorter concerning the standard battery box.

This study presents significant experimental findings, particularly for a standard battery box used in electrical vehicles, for the early detection and elimination of TR events in large-capacity LiFePO4 batteries. For passenger vehicles, the analysis contributes to enhance the predictive accuracy and provides a time guarantee for the preventative measure. It is recommended that the passengers to evacuate before the critical temperature to avoid possible subsequent TR reaction and risk. Meanwhile, the extinguishing device can be further improved to enhance the ability to detect fire at complex environment by deploying multi-coupled sensors and advanced control strategy, thereby improving the overall safety and reliability of energy storage systems.
Ruijie Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Cheng Chang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Key Research and Development Plan "High Power Lithium-ion battery energy storage Technology" Project number: 2022YFB2404800.
